Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of all over the place. I tried to dedicate about 26 hours a week to studying, but with limited resources at home and juggling extracurriculars, I had to be creative about when and where I could focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often spend time making diagrams and charts from the material, which took extra time but helped things stick. I'd usually study in longer blocks when I could find quiet time, rather than short daily sessions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd love to say I was consistent, but the reality is that deadlines drove most of my intense study sessions. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course material because other responsibilities piled up, and then I'd have to cram everything into a few days. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed to get through with everything else going on.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew there was something due soon or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, especially since my attendance wasn't great and I worried about falling behind. The peer support group I was part of would sometimes remind me about things posted on the platform, which helped prompt me to check in.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS did become part of my routine, especially when I was more engaged with the discussion forums. I'd log in to see what my peers were saying and respond to threads, which made it feel less like a chore. But honestly, that habit faded when I started feeling bored with the content and my motivation dropped. It became more sporadic after that.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me because I learn best visually. I could pause them, rewatch confusing parts, and take notes at my own pace. The discussion forums were also helpful because connecting with other students made the material feel more relevant. When peers explained things in their own words, it sometimes clicked better than the formal lectures.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading-heavy resources and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on a screen just made me zone out. I also didn't use any of the edutech tools that were suggested because I wasn't comfortable with them and didn't have time to figure them out. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content like videos or infographics always felt more valuable to me than walls of text. I also found value in peer interactions because they broke up the monotony and made learning feel more social and less isolating.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on, honestly. With my workload and everything else, I had to prioritize what was required just to pass. Required activities had clear stakes, so I made sure to complete those even when I wasn't feeling motivated. Optional stuff just felt like a luxury I didn't have the energy or time for, even if it might have helped.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study from those files later without being connected. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly. My LMS activity definitely doesn't capture all the hours I spent going over printed materials and my own notes.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying hard but it wouldn't show up in the system. Like when I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper based on downloaded content. I also studied with peers in person sometimes, going over concepts together, but none of that interaction showed up online. The LMS only sees what happens on the platform, not the real work happening elsewhere.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice, then download the slides and study from those for days afterward. I'd access a reading once to download it, then print it at the library when I could afford to. So my one-time access didn't reflect how much I actually engaged with that material over time.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where engaging felt really hard. When I was bored with the content or when stress from other areas of life piled up, I just couldn't bring myself to care about the coursework. My attendance dropped during those times, and I'd only do the bare minimum. The peer support helped a bit, but when my motivation was at rock bottom, even that wasn't enough to pull me back in.
Q13: All of those things affected how I used the LMS. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed with other responsibilities, I'd log in less frequently and rush through things without really absorbing them. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules altogether sometimes. Limited resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to, so I'd fall behind and then feel even more stressed trying to catch up.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd scroll through the LMS, click on videos or readings, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually learning anything. It happened most when I was bored or exhausted. I'd technically have activity logged, but I couldn't tell you what I actually absorbed during those sessions.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I didn't understand the instructions clearly. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because I didn't feel like I had anything valuable to contribute. I knew these things were important, but the combination of low motivation and feeling bored made it easy to push them off until the last possible moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly kick into gear, cramming everything into a short window. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and was pretty quiet otherwise. It wasn't a healthy pattern, but without that external pressure, I struggled to make myself engage with the material consistently.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got encouraging feedback, it gave me a small boost, but that feeling faded quickly. When feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, it sometimes made me want to disengage even more. I think I needed more consistent, supportive feedback to stay motivated, but with 18 online courses, that kind of personalized attention was rare.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which was both good and bad. Having clear deadlines helped me know what to prioritize, but the lack of flexibility made it hard when life got in the way. The way content was organized worked okay for my visual learning style, but I wished there were more interactive elements to break up the monotony and keep me engaged.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could learn at my own pace and revisit things. But the heavy reliance on text-based readings made it harder for me to stay focused. Also, not having built-in study groups or more structured peer activities meant I had to seek out that support on my own, which took extra effort. The peer support intervention helped, but I wish it had been more integrated from the start.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce quality work. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading discussions, genuinely trying to understand, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. High activity doesn't always mean comprehension or the ability to apply what you've learned. Sometimes you're just lost and searching for answers.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was low and I wasn't always active on the LMS, but I did a lot of studying offline and managed to pass. If someone looked at just my login frequency, they might think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in work in ways the system couldn't see. Some students are just more efficient or learn better outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and how long you stayed on a page. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or just had the tab open. They miss offline studying, peer discussions that happen outside the platform, and the mental effort you put into understanding something. They capture activity, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, asking questions, connecting ideas, and applying what you learn. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to other students and not feeling like I'm struggling alone. It's more than just showing up; it's being mentally present.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support outside the platform, or the hours I spent studying from printed materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture my emotional state, how boredom affected my motivation, or the financial constraints that limited my access sometimes. It also wouldn't show the effort it took just to keep going when I felt disconnected from the material. There's a whole person behind the data points.
